title: Bird Sound Identification Research
subtitle: Passive acoustic monitoring for avian conservation in Indonesia and Malaysia
reflection: I learned to fine-tune an established model (BirdNET) for a specific classification task rather than building from scratch.
I learned to process both sound data and image data — spectrograms taught me that audio and vision pipelines share more than I expected.
I learned to lead a team of capable students — capable people don't need management, they need direction and space.
I learned to present research at a conference in front of people who know the domain better than I do.

highlights: Took over as research lead. First task: build the dataset from scratch using Xeno-canto recordings.
Scoped to 219 species — specifically those endemic or endangered in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Borneo. 17 Borneo endemics. 18 IUCN-threatened.
Pulled 12,511 recordings + 8,840 negative samples (noise, speech, silence). Wrote the preprocessing code: 48 kHz resampling, log-mel spectrograms at 96 bins, 96x511 px per clip.
72/18/10 stratified split by species. No data leakage — split at recording level.
Fine-tuned BirdNET v2.4. Baseline accuracy on the endemic/endangered subset: 52%. After fine-tuning: 66%. F1 went from 0.29 to 0.42 — +44%.
Tested the model in the field: Bandung Zoo (Indonesia) and Malaysia National Zoo. Real animals, background noise, no controlled conditions.
Presented at ICITACEE 2025 in Semarang. Paper accepted and published in IEEE Xplore.

publications.title: Deep Learning for Bird Identification Using Sound: Enhancing Avian Monitoring and Conservation in Indonesia and Malaysia

highlights: Working with the lecturers to extend the paper for PeerJ submission.
Adding MixIT source separation — unsupervised, no clean reference signal needed. Targets overlapping bird calls in noisy recordings.
The hard problem: two birds calling at once over wind and insects. MixIT handles this without labeled pairs.
